transcript.text: Love your writing guidelines here.
We're seeing some night sweats examples pop up a little too often, so I'm tweaking that a little bit.
Cool.
Yeah, it's interesting.
It's kind of like I find these AI systems tend to just get repetitive at times, and it's like they beat kind of like the same thing.
So, yeah.
I guess as long as you put instructions around it.
Yeah, instructions can be helpful.
And then there's also like the underlying workflows, which just need a little bit more semantic work around kind of like fuzzy logic, basically.
Like, hey, you are not arbitrarily instructed to do X, Y, or Z.
You know, you have the freedom to choose.
And I think that's a lot of times where it gets hung up because it treats every instruction you give it as the word of, you know, the word from on high.
And you sort of have to give it permission to not do things in order to get it.
To be right.
And then we found that to be just, it's a good prompting in general, but it's very helpful for us because a lot of times what we'll say is, hey, we really don't like these things.
If you, you know, please don't do them.
But it's very hard to do the opposite where it's like, hey, please do these things, but not always.
You know, you don't have to do these things, but if appropriate, please do them.
And what's important generally to build in are what we call, well, what is called, we didn't call it, what is called an escape hatch.
So you basically have to build in the instructions, the ability for it to fail.
And you say, if you can do this, that's wonderful.
Great.
Please do it.
We want it to happen.
If you can't do it, don't fake it.
Don't artificially create a scenario in which you can do this.
You know, turn yourself into a pretzel trying to accomplish it.
Instead, just don't do it, right?
Or even just say, I can't do this.
And unless you do that.
It will always, always try to accomplish the task, and that obviously leads to weird, weird issues.
So that's our job, though.
We figure it out.
And then, of course, in the human review, we're catching that because we were just flagging it today to ourselves, like, hey, let's look at the end of this night sweats issue.
You know, that's why humans are still in the loop.
So, but yeah, so happy to chew through agendas today and anything else that you need to go over.
We obviously have some new articles in the pipe.
I'm happy to talk through anything you're seeing there.
And then we have some new ones.
I think we're waiting for Maggie's review on this latest batch, the very last batch of four.
But we already have an additional five.
And so we'll just keep queuing them.
No rush, you know, get through them when you can.
And then we'll review the updates.
Yeah, she just went through, I think, like, the round before.
Yeah.
If not mistaken.
So my sense is like, because she's, there's so much copy that we're like reviewing simultaneously outside of this workflow.
So she's kind of like, she's almost like oscillating between the two.
So essentially we have like website copy, email copy, this copy.
So it's just like a ton of copy.
Um, and then the other thing, especially like the latest batch that you sent over, some of it is like, she flagged me, like one of the articles is just like outside of her typical scope.
Um, and I think I mentioned this yesterday, but we signed up Liz's mom, who's a retired OB.
So she's going to go through and start reviewing content as well.
So I think we'll have, I think that'll be nice.
The other thing that, uh, won't need to go through, I think after you submit kind of like, okay, here are the articles with the updates, you know, based on.

The feedback, we're going to come up with almost like a matrix on our side as far as like who the like author is and then who the medical reviewer is because we'll basically have Joey and Liz, we'll have four people.

It's like Joey and Liz, two co-founders, we'll have Liz's mom, Nancy, as a medical reviewer, but also could be a writer as well as Maggie, who is sort of doing medical review, but can also be a writer as well.
So there's essentially like four people that we'll be able to sort of attach bylines to.
I don't know if we'll do it for all, but at least like we'll have a basket there and then we'll sort of determine who the medical reviewer is.
So we'll start to note those on those documents as you, as you send them over.
Okay, that's great.
And then as far as the public authors go, I think for your initial batch of launch content, this may be not strictly applicable or it may add additional.
complexity that's not really necessary at this stage.
However, I have been working on internally a project to add what we basically would refer to as like authors, you know, and they're not authors.
They're instead a framework for writing like a person.
I started with myself because I have, you know, 6,000 articles that I've written over the course of my career that are public.
And I was able to scrape them and then fine tune a model based on them.
And I'm finding that our internal writer is writing pretty much like me.
I don't like to admit it sometimes.
I'm like, really?
Really, dude?
But sometimes I have to be honest with myself.
Actually, it's probably true, you know, or close to true.

What that means for you, though, or any other client is that if you have somebody that has done a decent amount of writing, whether that's private or public, you know, or it doesn't have to be, it could be for medical journals, it could be in whatever contexts you
Or it doesn't have to be that person has actually written it.
We want to develop personas that are more generalized, but specific to certain client buckets.
As an example, just a step outside of your field of view, a security engineer, right?
Like we need to know like, okay, what is a really great security engineer write like and talk like and speak like?
And we can create a persona for that person.
Then we can say, oh, hey, you have three different clusters of content that you're going after.
One of them is really leaders, right?
Okay, let's talk in terms for them and create a voice that appeals to them.
And that's what our audience personas artifact is doing now.
But we think we just need to do more context engineering and be more specific about it.
And so what it would mean for you is if you have these authors, some of them may be, okay, we have an author that's an OB or XOB, you
They're going to write in a certain way for a certain audience that may feel totally different than the CEO of your company.
If you want to have a post from them, they're going to be talking about business realities or positioning in the market on a broader level.
And then OB might be talking more ground level advice that's just tangential to the product that you're making to establish authority and say, hey, this is great advice.
Oh, they have a product they make.
They probably are making a good product, you know?
And I think that's something we want to get more sophisticated about.
So it might be cool to do an exercise.
As soon as we get some tooling built internally, I'll flag it for you and say, hey, I think we're ready to start playing around with this.
And we can build out, we'll try one, you know, build out a persona for one of your authors and say, hey, this is what, you know, Maggie writes like, or this is what so-and-so writes like.
And like, let's, let's build out something for them and then tune it.
The bit against either comments or feedback or whatever, and then we're able to talk like them for their posts.
So instead of you kind of like tag, okay, cool, I think we're going to make the author this for this person.
It'd be like, hey, I need a cluster of content written by this author for this audience, and then we can generate that way.
So just wanted to flag that for you, something to think about.
Yeah, sounds great.
I had a chance to just briefly read through kind of the updates to the artifacts.
didn't read through the artifacts themselves, but I think for the most part, the diffs seem pretty good to me.

Or commenting, but I'm happy to convert to Notion for delivery on all of us.
That's totally fine.
Yeah, the reason why I say it is because I'm dropping them into Claude just by copying and pasting the text and it maintains the markup.
So that's the only...
Perfect.
Happy to do it.
Yeah, I'll make a note.
And then actually just a question for you is like, how often like when you're working with clients, are you like revisiting the artifacts?

Like as we, obviously if we're going through these like iterations with feedback, et cetera, is it like every pass you then update the artifacts or is like once a quarter, like what does that typical process look like?
Or do the artifacts become pretty like locked in at some point in time?
No, no, never, never.
Because they're living documents.
They have to update with the client's expectations on work their communications.
So right now, I'm updating these every almost...
Not...
Every day, but two or three times a week.
And obviously, you're seeing the dips of the changes that we've made.
Because you're giving such detailed feedback, which is wonderful, you would be shocked at how little feedback we sometimes get from clients.
We're like, please tell us anything.
And so it's really helpful what you're doing and what Maggie and other folks are doing, commenting these up, because it offers us a lot of guidance.
And so I'm updating the artifacts very regularly at this point.
At some point, that cadence will slow as we're like, you know, hey, these articles feel really great.
I saw this one thing here or there, but it's really more of like a one-off.
You know, okay, cool, we'll fix that, no problem.
And less of a rule or a guideline.
So that will slow down.
But in general, we find that monthly is about the lowest cadence we'd like to update the artifacts.
Because as we go through client calls, stuff comes up.
Oh, hey, we have a new product launch.
Great.
Let's get ready.
And for that product launch by, we don't even have to load the artifact, but let's update the artifact to be aware of that new product, right?
A new cluster of products you're offering because company context would contain, you know, all the products.
So if you're launching a new product line, we need to know what that is, ingest it, get it ready, get structure it, context engineer it for the company context.
And then audience personas, right?
You're like, hey, we discovered a new customer we didn't even know existed and we'd love to create a new cluster of content for them, right?
We need to create or update the audience personas artifact in order to reflect that.
So everything changes for a company.
If your company is stagnant, you're not growing and you're not changing, you're not reacting to the market.
Same thing for the artifacts.
They have to flow with that process.
So I would say monthly.
Okay.
That's helpful.
And then I think I shared with you what we were building on the website copy.
Did you incorporate that in this latest round of?
of the artifacts, or no, I just don't remember.
The ingredients, I think it was?
Not the ingredients, the PDP copy that we've been working on.
No, we haven't yet.
So that'll be in the next round.
I'll actually make a note of that, because I had put it in our file to update, but I don't think we tackled it on this one, so.
Okay.
Yeah, because I think there's stuff in there that actually will be quite helpful.
the context matters, that it's, like, on a product page, but I think it'll be, there's, like, certain word choice that Maggie's using, and that I think will be quite helpful.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, we'll make sure that we get integrate that.
That shouldn't be a problem for this week, so by next week, we should have it integrated.
Okay.
I guess the only other, like, item that I was kind of curious about is Ada was working on kind of those ingredient pages.
Pages, like if there's any kind of like updates there to talk about?

Okay.

So we have like a number of video shoots and photo shoots that are occurring.
Yeah.
With one of them scheduled, I think it's like September 6th or 7th.
And so it takes like two weeks to get those assets retouched, et cetera.
Yep.

My guess is it'll be ready to go, yeah, August 19th.
Okay.
That's cool.
The website or the CMS access is one question.
The other question related to this, which is really probably one of the important ones for us, is just.
And structure.

Because right now we're not running internal linking workflows on any of these articles.

Like your mention of, you know, Afron links to our Afron ingredient page, right?
And et cetera.
And so if all we need really is the breadcrumbs, like what is the structure of the site look like?
And then we'll write a script that pre-populates those links, you know, biologica.com, whatever your TLD is, and then forward slash blog, forward slash ingredients, whatever it is.
And then we'll populate all of those links so that when we are publishing to the CMS, we're not having to then retcon links back in, you know, that way when you go live and your pages start.
Or to get indexed, Google is like, oh, cool, this is a real site.
You know, it'll index them all.
You want to do that versus having to go back and then have it re-index pages just because you've added links to them.
So we want to be ready.
Yeah, what I'll probably do, I mean, would it be helpful for me just to connect you with the development shop and just, you can just, like, ask them some of those questions directly?
Yeah, any load I can take off of you, you don't have to play telephone, you know, happy to talk to them.
It doesn't even have to be, like, I don't have to hop on a call, we can async, whatever.
But you can connect in whatever way works for you and then we'll figure it out with them and they'll know, of course, they'll have in their head what they're doing.
And I'm sure it's well documented.
So we're happy to get that info.
Okay.
Yeah, I'll just connect you after this call with them.
It's really just mechanical stuff, right?
It's not so much philosophy.
Because that's already been decided at some point.
It's more mechanically.
However, I will flag, like, if we see some sort of glaring issue where we're like, hey, this flies in the face of every best practice, I'll let you know.
You know, we'll be like, you know, our SEO team is like, why are you doing this?
And you may have a reason.
And that's cool.
We just want to be a good partner and flag it, you know, because we see.
Yeah, I don't think, like, the team that we're working with is, like, super low ego.
Like, I can't imagine the way that they're doing it would be strange.
But certainly they're, like, very amenable to be like, oh, yeah, we're happy to make a tweak or whatever.
Yeah, yeah.
I'll make sure, obviously, that flows through you so that, you you have eyes on it.
You can make the final call.
Just if we have any recs or anything, I'll flag it.
And it may not.
You know, I'm sure it'll be just straightforward best practices stuff.
But every once in while, just to let you know, we've seen clients stuff, we're like, ooh, you know.
Honestly, this is going to be hard for us to deliver on our goals for you because this is standing in our way.
And so.
We like to flag that stuff from just a selfish aspect, but also in a normal best practices way.
Sometimes people do some weird stuff for very non-obvious reasons.
And it's like, why is this?
And they're like, I don't know why we do it this way.
He's like, oh, well, here's a suggestion.
We'll let you know if we see anything like that.
Okay.
I think that's it for me.
I don't know if there's anything else that you wanted to chat through or had questions on.
Yeah, nothing glaring.
We're going to keep going.
We have another batch of five.
And once again, no pressure.
We'll just keep the queue rich for you.

I'm also working on a better layout for the Airtable for the Content OS because I just think something a little cleaner where you have a view of just stuff to review and you can go through it and take it to send it on forward in the queue.
We'll update that.
And if we do, I'll let you know in the next call.
But other than that, we'll just keep delivering and chewing on things.
I'll work on the Fact Checker, and we'll update you as soon as we know that.
We'll just process all the ingredient pages you gave us and then get you those back.
And then if you have additional pages or anything you'd like to run through it, we can.
I guess the last question I have, so you'll send five more over that obviously will incorporate kind of the latest revisions to the artifacts.
Yeah, everything.
At what point, like, because there certainly is, I haven't taken a step back to look at, like, all the content that's been written.
We obviously have some goals around, like, the various topics that we want content in ahead of launch.
You know, we're, call it two months out at this point.
If you just run some simple math, say it's, like, five articles a week, that's, call it 20-ish articles a month.
Call it an additional 40 articles, give or take.
So that puts us at roughly, like, 50 articles, I think, where, so far, they've been delivered.
Which feels like a pretty good amount to launch.

I guess I'm just curious, like, is there an opportunity at some point to, like, increase the velocity in the sense that, like, okay, we just need to make sure that we have however many articles written across all these topics?
Like, if the artifacts are good, can you just hit go and, like, boom, you have, here's 100, like, go review them at your leisure?
Or do you like to go with this still at the stage, this, like, methodical approach around, let's do a few, let's capture feedback, to incorporate it?
I guess, like, what's the best way to ensure that, like, you guys deliver the highest quality, but we still get the, like, the critical mass that we're kind of looking for?
Yeah, it's logarithmic, right?
It's definitely not a linear curve.
At the beginning, we like this cadence because the degree...
ease of change and the directionality are large, right?
You're getting like a lot of, there's a big delta between what you would love to see quality-wise and what we're actually delivering.
That's why we're getting a lot of comments and markups and things like that.
As that cadence slows and narrows, we're like, cool, it's time to crank up the volume knob, right?
So I could generate 100 articles for you today, right, and deliver them to you today.
But they would be, like, the quality would be rough because we'd end up having to incorporate so much feedback.
And then it's like, okay, well, then I'm 20 articles in and I can see that it's not matching quality.
That's the only thing we want to avoid just because it's labor on your part and our part that's not necessary.
So let's say we'll keep this rough cadence, let's say five a week for another two weeks.
And then let's see how the markups come back.
And if we feel like we're getting closer, then we'll just up the scale, right?
And we can increase the amount delivered to...
To a certain degree, we like to maintain a rough cadence because the human intervention stuff is important.
You know, there is just fine-tuning that we do at the end.
What you're seeing is not coming directly out of our pipeline, right?
I have reviewed all of these articles.
I read every word, right?
So it's not, you know, if there's anything that's screwed up in there, it's because I missed it or because I don't know the medical context and that's the valuable information we're getting from you.
So we're working on the artifacts right now to get that delta narrowed and then we'll increase production.
I would say on average, you would want to produce, like, let's say we're at speed.
Let's say it's six months from now and we're still making content for you.
Everything's cruising, the website's live, we're seeing some traffic and we're continuing to produce.
We probably want to shoot for a cadence of, like, pieces a week-ish because it's not really necessary to do more than that.
A, for review pipes.
But then also indexing.
Like Google doesn't index 100 pages in a week.
They don't care.
In fact, you can't hurt site health.
So you just want to like ship things on a regular cadence really.
Now, if it's a bigger project that's something that's more programmatic, we can talk about ways to deploy pages and then index them systematically over time.
Like for instance, if you, we had a new project that was like some sort of directory or hub or data driven project that was more of a PSEO type engagement.

Hey, we have a database of 250 data pieces that we'd love to ship a page of content for each of those, you know, items and fill that out programmatically with generated content that's reviewed and then shipped.
We do this for clients all the time, directories of apps or, you know, lists of, well, lists of glossary terms, right?
As an example, that's a little different.
Because it requires less human intervention.
It's a lot easier to build the rails for that sort of thing.
And then you would have like a hygiene thing where you would just submit like a dozen pages a week to Google for indexing.
And it would take a while, but you can do it.
But as far as like editorial content, like we're producing for you, probably should ship about 10 pieces a week no more.
Yeah, I I think even for us, like, aside from the human intervention part, it's like at 40 articles a month, it's just like a lot of content for like our article.
Like you would come back and be like, do you actually need five, 600, 700 articles?
Like, maybe, but yeah, sort of like narrow niche at the moment.
It'll get to some part where it's sort of like, there's just no reason.
Yeah, it's the editorial content is interesting because I think.
It's really about like timeliness.
And so you end up having a base coding of content that you need to establish yourself and to allow the fingers of Google to tickle you, you know, and like, that's all great.
But then beyond that, it's really about like, hey, we need, we'll have content needs that are driven by the company's needs, you know, new product launches, new positioning.
We have a new campaign that's going out that's targeting a specific individual or stage of life or whatever.
And then you'll build content to support those as pillars.
And that's great.
But beyond that, like, I think beyond your initial establishment, you should really think about it in terms of link magnets or attention magnets.
So, hey, what's a project that we can create that is more of a, like a use-based or value-based project, right?
So what is like a thing that would be hugely valuable?
Like, is that, like, you go out there and you're like, wow, I wish I had, like, a...
Searchable database that returned, like, every drug that affects body temperature or whatever, right?
Like, there's just the ones that I found out that are either paywalled or inaccurate or clunky, you know, just bad design sometimes, right?
And then you create a product, basically a tool that people can access and use, and then you create content around that tool to help people find the tool.
Because it may be, like, you know, Ben Easy for Body Heat, Sapphire for Body Heat, whatever, I'm not, not that these are applicable, we'll just give you an example.
And then you build that tool into the page, right?
You embed the tool in the page, and then people start to find the tool, and it's useful.
Maybe it's even a shareable tool, you know, something that they can even share a card out, things like that.

Now you're building something that's more of what we call an organic link magnet, which offers people the ability to say, like, hey, I'm going to embed this on my page, I'm going to link back to a, like, oh, if you want to know what ingredients are.
Great for what?
This tool is really awesome, you know, and some help writers linking to you, et cetera.
This is so much more effective than link purchasing or, you know, kind of link harvesting.
It's just back linking is one of those things that it's like if you pay for it, it's almost never worth the money.
You know, sometimes it can be very early on, very early on.
You can pay for some links.
But in general, organic links are valued so much higher.
It's, it's like pennies on the dollar, you know, in terms of it.
So, anyhow, that's just something to throw out there.
Like other pillars of content coming down the road should be like service oriented, everything.
Cool.
I got to jump to another call, but, yeah, just let me know after you drop those updated artifacts in Notion so I can update stuff on my side, but.
Yeah, we'll update the, the artifacts to Notion format and the diffs as well.
So you'll have it all.
Okay.
All right.
Great.
Thank you.
Ciao.
Yeah.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Weekly sync between growthx.ai and Hellobdv to discuss content strategy, website launch, and ongoing collaboration.](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Content production to increase gradually as quality aligns with expectations (currently \~5 articles/week, aiming for \~10/week)](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=3.0)
  - [Website alpha delivery on Aug 19, potential full launch Sept 5-24; CMS access likely available Aug 19](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=9.0)
  - [Artifacts being updated frequently (2-3x/week) based on detailed feedback; will incorporate new PDP copy](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=502.0)
  - [New fact-checking workflow for ingredient pages expected this week](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=784.0)

## Topics

### Content Production and Quality

  - [Currently producing \~5 articles/week, will increase to \~10/week as quality improves](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=1308.0)
  - [Aiming for \~50 articles at launch, with potential for 40 more in the next two months](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=1420.0)
  - [Focus on maintaining quality while increasing volume; avoiding premature mass production](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=1339.0)
  - [Long-term strategy to include more service-oriented content and "organic link magnets"](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=1593.0)

### Website Launch and Structure

  - [Alpha delivery: August 19](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=873.0)
  - [Potential full launch: September 5-24 (company launch on 24th)](https://fathom.video/share/qZDHxKUTVozSRLgSWUy4zYWmX3toS7VA?tab=summary&timestamp=892.0)
